a: A dbt model is just a SELECT query saved in a .sql file. When you run dbt, it takes that SELECT and builds a real table or view in your warehouse for you.
fs: A model is a SELECT statement; dbt wraps it in CREATE TABLE/VIEW so you only write transformation logic.
fj: Thinking a model is a special new language. It is plain SQL SELECT, one file per model.

q: What does ref() do in dbt?
a: ref() lets one model point to another model by name instead of typing the table name. dbt uses these links to figure out the right build order and insert the correct schema automatically.
fs: ref() builds the dependency graph (DAG) and resolves real table names, so models run in the correct order.
fj: Hardcoding the warehouse table name instead of ref(). Then dbt cannot order builds and breaks across environments.

q: What is a source in dbt?
a: A source is the raw data already loaded into your warehouse by some other tool. You declare it in a YAML file, then read from it with source() instead of writing the raw table name directly.
fs: Sources name your raw input tables in one place, so downstream models reference them cleanly and freshness can be checked.
fj: Confusing a source with a model. A source is raw input dbt did not build; a model is something dbt builds.

a: Materialization is how dbt saves your model's result in the warehouse. The two basic choices are 'view' (saves only the query) and 'table' (saves the actual data as a real table).
fs: Materialization picks the build strategy: view, table, incremental, or ephemeral, trading freshness against speed and cost.
fj: Assuming every model becomes a table. By default many are views, which re-run their query each time you read them.

a: A test is a check that confirms your data is correct, like 'this column is never null' or 'every id is unique'. dbt runs the check and fails loudly if the data breaks the rule.
fs: Tests are assertions on data quality; built-ins like unique and not_null catch bad data before it reaches dashboards.
fj: Thinking tests check your SQL code logic. They check the actual rows in the warehouse, not the code itself.
